one_line_takeaway: By repurposing certified-robustness (randomized smoothing) guarantees as an inference-stage indistinguishability bound, Robust Privacy cuts black-box model-inversion attack success from 73% to 4% and dominates DP-SGD and randomized response in the privacy-utility tradeoff, while explicitly not protecting against aggregate function-level model distillation.
key_finding: Attribute-level: RP increases the median RAP-compatible age-inference interval from 23.50 years (unprotected baseline) to 26.70-27.73 years at sigma=0.6 and to 28.37-29.96 years at sigma=1.0 (N=1000 and N=5000 respectively), while retaining 81.95-92.86% test accuracy depending on the sigma/N setting (base model itself hits 100% accuracy/AUC 1.00). Instance-level: on the label-only black-box FaceNet64 inversion attack, RP reduces attack success rate (ASR) from an unprotected 73% down to 4% at sigma=0.1, N=100 (with non-trivial residual accuracy), and to 44% at sigma=0.03, N=100 while accuracy remains 100%. In the privacy-utility comparison, RP at sigma=0.05, N=100 retains 98.4% accuracy at 21% ASR, versus the best high-utility DP-SGD point at 97.3% accuracy / 44% ASR, and randomized response at 95.0% accuracy / 77% ASR; DP-SGD only reaches a comparable 17% ASR to RP's 95.0%-accuracy/17%-ASR point by dropping accuracy to 61.7%. The paper also notes that at the DP-SGD noise multipliers used, the formal privacy budget epsilon spans 3.25x10^6 to 1.15x10^8 -- far outside the regime where DP composition gives a non-vacuous guarantee -- which the authors argue is a structural (not tuning) limitation reflecting the training-stage/inference-stage channel mismatch. Increasing the Monte Carlo sample size N at fixed sigma improves privacy and utility simultaneously (not a tradeoff) in both the attribute-inference and inversion experiments, at the cost of higher per-query inference compute. Scope-boundary finding: against DisGUIDE model distillation on CIFAR-10, RP's effect on distilled-student accuracy tracks the RP-protected teacher's own accuracy degradation rather than independently suppressing the attack -- i.e., mitigation appears driven by target-model utility loss rather than by the per-query masking mechanism, confirming that RP does not directly counter function-level/aggregate extraction the way it does per-query attribute- and instance-level leakage.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Robust Privacy (RP), an inference-stage privacy notion that reinterprets certified robustness (specifically randomized smoothing) as an indistinguishability guarantee: if a classifier's prediction at input x is certifiably invariant within a radius-R neighborhood with confidence at least 1-alpha, then x satisfies (R, alpha)-Robust Privacy, and the paper proves (Theorem 1) that any adversary observing only the released prediction has distinguishing advantage at most alpha/2 between x and any neighbor x' within distance R. Building on RP, the authors formalize Robust Attribute Privacy (RAP, Definition 2), which characterizes the set of sensitive-attribute values compatible with a released prediction given fixed side information x_-1, and prove (Theorem 2) that an (R, alpha)-RP certificate implies the RAP-compatible set contains a sub-interval of length 2R around the true attribute value with confidence 1-alpha. They instantiate RP operationally via Gaussian randomized smoothing (Cohen et al.) and empirically evaluate it against three inference-interface attacks: attribute inference, black-box model inversion, and model distillation (used as a boundary/negative case to delineate RP's scope).
threat_model: Black-box adversary with hard-label-only access to a deployed model's prediction API -- no access to confidence scores, gradients, or model parameters. Three attacker capabilities are modeled: (i) Scenario I, Sensitive Attribute Inference -- the attacker knows all non-target attributes x_-1 (a standard assumption in prior attribute-inference work) and observes one released prediction for a target user, attempting to narrow the plausible range of an unknown sensitive attribute (e.g., age, BMI) via repeated queries with candidate values; (ii) Scenario II, Model Inversion -- the attacker iteratively submits synthetic inputs and uses label-change feedback (the Kahla et al. label-only attack) to optimize toward the training distribution and reconstruct representative training instances (evaluated on face recognition, CelebA); (iii) Scenario III, Model Distillation -- the attacker issues many queries and trains a student model (DisGUIDE, hard-label data-free distillation) to approximate the deployed model's global input-output function; explicitly treated as a scope boundary rather than a target RP is designed to stop, since it is a function-level/aggregate leakage channel rather than a per-query one.
limitations: The paper explicitly frames model distillation/extraction as a negative result / scope boundary: RP masks per-query, fine-grained input-output dependence but does not suppress function-level extraction learned in aggregate across many queries, and the observed mitigation there is confounded with utility degradation rather than a targeted privacy effect. The RP certificate as instantiated (randomized smoothing) is probabilistic (failure probability alpha) and geometric in the raw input space; the paper notes the operational meaning of the radius R differs across settings (a direct attribute-level reading in the tabular case via Theorem 2 vs. a higher-dimensional 'candidate set' reading in the image case) and does not claim a universal interpretation. Composition of RP with DP (e.g., training under DP then serving under RP) is stated as an open direction left to future work, not evaluated. The DP-SGD baseline is run at noise multipliers that push epsilon into a regime (10^6-10^8) where the authors themselves state the formal DP guarantee 'no longer carries quantitative meaning,' which they justify as reflecting real deployability constraints in the literature but which also means the comparison is not an apples-to-apples comparison of two guarantees with matched formal strength. Abstention handling differs by experiment (reported/required in the classification experiment for a well-formed certificate, disabled via an always-return-a-label protocol in the inversion/distillation experiments), which the paper defends as necessary to avoid a trivial defense via query refusal but which also means the certified (R, alpha)-RP guarantee itself is not being reported for the inversion/distillation attack-success numbers.

datasets_used: Medical Insurance Cost Prediction dataset (100,000 records; attribute-inference experiment)
CelebA (1000 private identities; model-inversion experiment, FaceNet64 target / FaceNet evaluator)
CIFAR-10 (ResNet34-8x teacher; model-distillation boundary experiment, via DisGUIDE)
quantitative_results: Median RAP-compatible age-inference interval: 23.50 years (unprotected baseline) vs 29.96 years (RP, sigma=1.0, N=5000)
Attribute-inference test accuracy: 84.21% (RP, sigma=1.0, N=5000) vs 100.00% (unprotected base classifier, AUC 1.00)
Model inversion ASR: 73% (unprotected) -> 4% (RP, sigma=0.1, N=100) and 44% (RP, sigma=0.03, N=100, accuracy 100%)
Privacy-utility comparison: RP 98.4% accuracy at 21% ASR (sigma=0.05, N=100) vs DP-SGD 97.3% accuracy at 44% ASR vs randomized response 95.0% accuracy at 77% ASR
DP-SGD must drop to 61.7% accuracy to reach 17% ASR, while RP reaches 17% ASR at 95.0% accuracy (sigma=0.06, N=100)
DP-SGD privacy budget at evaluated noise multipliers: epsilon from 3.25x10^6 (sigma_SGD=0.05) to 1.15x10^8 (sigma_SGD=0.01), delta=1e-5
Certified robust radius R (attribute-inference setting): grows from 4.15 to 13.10 (N=1000) and 4.97 to 14.06 (N=5000) as sigma goes 0.1->1.0
